coredata.dc:title: A disposable electrochemical sensor for amyloid-β42 protein based on molecular imprinted polymers with nitrogen doped carbon dots-graphene nanohybrid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pakapongpan, Saithip
Poo-arporn, Yingyot
Ninket, Sunatcha
Poo-arporn, Rungtiva P.
coredata.dc:description:
This work presented a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) electrochemical sensor for determination of amyloid-β42 (Aβ42) as an Alzheimer’s disease (AD) biomarker. A nitrogen-doped carbon-dot-graphene (NCD-G) nanohybrid was synthesized through a simple ultrasonic method. Polypyrrole (PPY) was used as an imprinted polymer with an Aβ42 template for the electropolymerization process. The NCD-G and MIP were modified on a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) without binding agent. The morphology and electrochemical properties of MIP/NCD-G modified SPCE were studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–Vis spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV). The developed sensor exhibited good linear response in a range from 5 to 70 pg/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 pg/mL under optimal conditions. This sensor shows excellent performance due to the enhancement of effective surface area, electrical conductivity, and electrocatalytic activity. This MIP sensor takes advantage of molecularly imprinted technique combined with nanohybrid to achieve high selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, it possessed good reproducibility and miniaturization that could be applied as a platform for portable devices in healthcare in the future.
Novelty statement This work provides the combination of a zero-dimensional and two-dimensional materials, nitrogen doped carbon dots-graphene nanohybrid (NCD-G) by self-assembly through a simple ultrasonication method and applied as a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) sensor for determination of amyloid-β42 protein. We propose a minimum number of preparation steps which NCD-G could enhance the MIP sensor performance, owing to the synergistic effects with high selectivity and sensitivity. The hybrid formation of the carbon dots with graphene using their π-π interactions in carbon nanostructures. NCD-G nanohybrid and MIP modified on screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) for the determination of amyloid-β42 have not been previously investigated. Moreover, this disposable strip is a single-use tool that might be essential for on-site devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amyloid β42
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Graphene
Carbon dots
Electrochemical sensor
Nitrogen doped

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of the molar ratio of template to monomer
The effect of elution time and rebinding time
Calibration curve for electrochemical determination of Aβ42
Analytical application of the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE in artificial human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) graphene, (B) NCD-G, (C) MIP/NCD-G, (D) NIP/NCD-G and (E and F) TEM images of NCD.
(A) UV–Vis spectrum of NCD. (B) The CV curves of bare SPCE (a), G/SPCE (b), NCD/SPCE (c) and NCD-G/SPCE (d) to [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− 2.5 mM.
XPS high-resolution spectra of C 1s (A), N 1s (B), and O 1s (C) of NCD.

(A) The SWV curves of MIP/NCD-G/SPCE (B) NIP/NCD-G/SPCE before (red) after (green) removal of Aβ42; after rebinding (blue) of Aβ42 100 pg/mL in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.0 containing 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl. (C) The peak current variations of the imprinted (MIP) and the non-imprinted (NIP) film electrode. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Effect of electropolymerization cycles on current response; (B) Effect of the ratio of template molecules to functional monomers on current response; (C) Effect of extraction time on current response; (D) Effect of rebinding incubation time on current response.

(A) The plot of the current response (ΔI) to concentration of Aβ42 with 15 electrodes. (B) The SWV curves of MIP/NCD-G/SPCE in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.0 containing [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at various of Aβ42 concentration. (C) The calibration plot of ΔI versus the concentration of Aβ42 at the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE. (D) The selectivity of the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE to Aβ42 with different interferences: BNP, IgG, and HSA.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of NCD-G/SPCE, the MIP preparation of MIP/NCD-G/SPCE and the detection process.
Determination of Aβ42 in artificial serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A disposable electrochemical sensor for amyloid-β42 protein based on molecular imprinted polymers with nitrogen doped carbon dots-graphene nanohybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Opto-Electrochemical Sensing Research Team, National Electronics and Computer Technology Center, National Science and Technology Development Agency, Pathum Thani 12120, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Electronics and Computer Technology Center
National Science and Technology Development Agency

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Opto-Electrochemical Sensing Research Team, National Electronics and Computer Technology Center, National Science and Technology Development Agency, Pathum Thani 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Synchrotron Light Research Institute (Public Organization), Nakhon Ratchasima 30000, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Synchrotron Light Research Institute (Public Organization), Nakhon Ratchasima 30000, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biological Engineering Program, Faculty of Engineering, King Mongkut’s University of Technology Thonburi, Bangkok 10140, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: King Mongkut’s University of Technology Thonburi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biological Engineering Program, Faculty of Engineering, King Mongkut’s University of Technology Thonburi, Bangkok 10140, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presented a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) electrochemical sensor for determination of amyloid-β42 (Aβ42) as an Alzheimer’s disease (AD) biomarker. A nitrogen-doped carbon-dot-graphene (NCD-G) nanohybrid was synthesized through a simple ultrasonic method. Polypyrrole (PPY) was used as an imprinted polymer with an Aβ42 template for the electropolymerization process. The NCD-G and MIP were modified on a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) without binding agent. The morphology and electrochemical properties of MIP/NCD-G modified SPCE were studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–Vis spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV). The developed sensor exhibited good linear response in a range from 5 to 70 pg/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 pg/mL under optimal conditions. This sensor shows excellent performance due to the enhancement of effective surface area, electrical conductivity, and electrocatalytic activity. This MIP sensor takes advantage of molecularly imprinted technique combined with nanohybrid to achieve high selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, it possessed good reproducibility and miniaturization that could be applied as a platform for portable devices in healthcare in the future.

This work provides the combination of a zero-dimensional and two-dimensional materials, nitrogen doped carbon dots-graphene nanohybrid (NCD-G) by self-assembly through a simple ultrasonication method and applied as a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) sensor for determination of amyloid-β42 protein. We propose a minimum number of preparation steps which NCD-G could enhance the MIP sensor performance, owing to the synergistic effects with high selectivity and sensitivity. The hybrid formation of the carbon dots with graphene using their π-π interactions in carbon nanostructures. NCD-G nanohybrid and MIP modified on screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) for the determination of amyloid-β42 have not been previously investigated. Moreover, this disposable strip is a single-use tool that might be essential for on-site devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Amyloid β (Aβ) is a small 39–43 amino acid residue derived from the amyloid precursor protein in the brain, which is one of the potential biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease (AD). AD is a severe neurodegenerative disorder caused by the plaques in the brain, which leads to progressive memory, cognitive impairment and personality changes, with evolution to dementia and death [1]. Aβ has several types of isoforms, amyloid β42 protein (Aβ42) is a polypeptide containing 42 amino acids, which is the most commonly regarded as one of the main hallmarks in the brain. That protein can become senile plaque in the brain tissue and block neuronal transmission, which can cause cell death. The Aβ42 level in cerebrospinal fluid of AD patients is usually lower than a normal (500–700 pg/mL, 110–155 pM) due to its aggregation [2,3]. Therefore, the low-level Aβ42 below 192 pg/mL is commonly considered a promising biomarker for AD diagnosis. The approaches for the detection of Aβ are including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) [4], immunoassays [5,6], spectrophotometry [7], fluorescence imaging [8] and electrochemiluminescence techniques [9,10]. However, these techniques have certain disadvantages, such as high costs, complicated procedures, long analysis times, requires the use of relatively expensive antibodies, and numerous interfering variables. Among a competitive screening technique, the electrochemical method is considered to develop a simple, rapid, inexpensive and highly sensitive method to detect Aβ42 with alternative affinity such as aptamers [11], DNA [12], and molecularly imprinted polymers [13,14] that have been utilized to achieve the desired result, but the lack of selectivity limits their application. In particular, molecularly imprinted polymer-based sensors have been extensively employed. Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) is usually synthesized by the polymerization of functional monomers and cross-linkers of a template molecule as specific recognition sites, and the special tailor-made binding sites can be obtained after the removal of template molecules. These specific receptor sites have high selective recognition to the template molecules over its structurally related compounds [15–18]. MIP has many advantages over other selective biosensors such as chemical and thermal stability, physical robustness, simplicity and low cost of preparation that are appropriate in sensing applications. Several studies have been published on the preparation of MIP based electrochemical sensors for the determination of various proteins and substances, for example cortisol [19,20], cytokine IL-1β [21], glycoprotein [22], dopamine [23] and hemoglobin [24]. Consequently, there is still a need to improve the simplicity, conductivity and sensitivity of MIP sensors for quantitative analysis and make them more affordable and the diagnosis more reliable. There are several studies using nanomaterials particularly carbon materials such as graphene, CNT and carbon dots, that have been widely utilized to increase the current signal to get more response and the total surface-to-volume ratio surface area of the active electrode surface. Carbon quantum dots (CD), a significant class of carbon nanomaterial with sizes less than 10 nm and have an alternative to properties such as unique quantum confinement, high photostability, high conductivity, low toxicity, electrocatalytic activity, and biocompatibility [25,26].

Previous reports have demonstrated the use of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCD) as modifier material has led to enhanced electrochemical properties [27,28]. Recently, Fu, L. et al. 2018 [29] reported that NCD modified glassy carbon electrodes can enhance the electrocatalytic activity of paracetamol and hydrogen peroxide. Carbon dots and graphene composites were reported in Gomes, 2019 et al. work, with hydrothermal synthesis and applied as supercapacitor [30]. NCD not only serves as a fundamental assistant for immobilizing with screen printed electrodes (SPCE) but also plays a key role for the enhancement of sensitivity and stability due to its electrode properties. Thus, carbon-dot-graphene nanohybrid could serve as a probe signal with superior catalytic activity, unique electric conductivity, and a well-defined configuration. The performance of these MIP-based electrochemical sensors is further enhanced.

This nanohybrid is used as a sensor for the determination of amyloid-β42 protein. NCD not only prevent graphene sheets from aggregating and improve their solubility but also reduce double-layer capacitance signals. Graphene contributes to electrical conductivity and increases the specific surface area. The NCD-G nanohybrid enhances MIP sensor performance due to its synergistic effects providing high selectivity and sensitivity. The hybrid formation of carbon dots with graphene involves π-π interactions within carbon nanostructures. Consequently, an electrochemical sensor based on an MIP with NCD-G-modified screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) has been demonstrated for the highly sensitive and selective determination of Aβ42. The NCD-G nanohybrid was prepared and then polymerized with polypyrrole (PPY) to form an MIP film with an Aβ42 template. The electrochemical performance of the sensor was investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV). The MIP/NCD-G/SPCE was applied for the determination of Aβ42, using ferri-ferrocyanide ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−) as a redox probe. Moreover, the use of NCD-G nanohybrid and MIP on SPCE for the determination of amyloid-β42 has not been previously explored. This proposed sensor represents a valuable tool for disposable Alzheimer's disease diagnostics, offering high selectivity and cost-effectiveness.

Recently, a few reports have employed the composites of graphene and NCD as reinforcement materials to prepare MIP. This approach combines a zero-dimensional and two-dimensional material, nitrogen doped carbon dots-graphene nanohybrid (NCD-G) by self-assembly through a simple ultrasonication method and is applied as a MIP sensor for the determination of amyloid-β42 protein. NCD not only helps the graphene sheet from aggregation and improves the solubility but also reduces the double layer capacitance signal. Graphene is responsible for the electrical conductivity and increases the specific surface area. NCD-G could enhance the MIP sensor performance, owing to the synergistic effects with high selectivity and sensitivity. The hybrid formation of carbon dots and graphene involves π-π interactions in their carbon nanostructures. Hence, the fabrication of an electrochemical sensor based on MIP with NCD-G modified SPCE was demonstrated for the highly sensitive and selective determination of Aβ42. NCD-G nanohybrid was prepared and then followed through polymerization assembled with PPY to form MIP film with Aβ42 template. The electrochemical sensor performance was investigated by CV and SWV. The prepared MIP/NCD-G/SPCE was applied to the determination of Aβ42 with ferry-ferrocyanide ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−) as a redox probe. Moreover, NCD-G nanohybrid and MIP modified on SPCE for the determination of amyloid-β42 have not been previously investigated. This proposed sensor provides a very useful tool for disposable AD diagnostics that is highly selective and inexpensive.

Amyloid-β42 (Aβ42), graphene sheet, urea, pyrrole, N–dimethylformamide (DMF), dopamine (DA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), oxalic acid, O-Phenylenediamine (O-PD), Potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) (K3[Fe(CN)6]), Potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) (K4[Fe (CN)6]), Potassium chloride (KCl), brain natriuretic peptide (BNP), immunoglobulin G (IgG) and human serum albumin (HSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The membranes (nylon, 0.22 μm, made in Jinlong) and Silica gel 60 (mesh 70–230) were purchased from Merck. All solvents including ethanol and petroleum ether were purchased from Labscan.

In brief, NCD was synthesized using the hydrothermal method partially based on the procedure reported by Arkin et al. [31] (see Scheme 1 ). 0.8 g of urea and 0.5 g of O-PD were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water and stirred for 5 min to form a homogeneous mixture solution. This mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. The resulting solution was filtered through a nylon microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm and dialyzed in DI water using a dialysis membrane with an MWCO of 1000 Da for 1 day. The final NCD solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL was stored at 4 °C.

NCD-G was prepared by adding 10 mg of graphene powder into 20 mL of NCD solution. The mixture solution was sonicated for 10 h. After that, the dispersion was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and washed with DI water three times. Finally, the NCD-G was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

The preparation of the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE sensor was shown in Scheme 1. 1 μL of NCD-G dispersion (0.1 mg/mL) was dropped onto the SPCE surface and dried under room temperature. Subsequently, the NCD-G/SPCE was washed with DI water to remove any unbound NCD-G. PPY was electropolymerized as MIP film on the surface of NCD-G/SPCE by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The NCD-G/SPCE substrate was used as a base for electropolymerization of pyrrole monomers, generating an imprint site between monomers and Aβ42. Thirty microliters of 50 μg/mL Aβ42 in 0.1 M PBS, pH of 7.0, were incubated on the NCD-G/SPCE for 5 min. Next, 10 μL of 40 mM pyrrole monomer was dropped and incubated for an additional 5 min before electropolymerization. CV was employed to carry out the electropolymerization process in the potential range for 3 cycles in the potential range of 0 to +0.8 V with a scan rate of 0.1 V/s. The remaining unreacted monomers were then removed using CV under the same conditions in 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.0. Finally, the Aβ42 template was removed by eluting with 5 mL of 5 mM oxalic acid for 10 min and subsequent washing with DI water. A non-molecularly imprinted polymer (NIP) was prepared under identical conditions, excluding the Aβ42 template, to serve as a control. Oxalic acid is a good elution reagent that is able to destroy the electrostatic force between Aβ and pyrrole. For comparison, a non-imprinted (NIP) modified electrode was prepared by the same as the above conditions, without the addition of the Aβ42 template in the electro-polymerization process.

The electrochemical was characterized using the cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique with a potential range of 0 to +0.8 V at a scan rate of 100 mV/s. For the Aβ42 detection step, MIP and NIP were incubated with 50 μL of 100 pg/mL of Aβ42 for 10 min, followed by a wash with 5 mL of 1 mM oxalic acid for 1 min to remove unbound Aβ42. The investigation of current response was carried out using the SWV with a range between –0.3 V and + 0.6 V, a pulse amplitude of 0.05 V, a step potential of 0.05, and a modulation time of 0.05 s, at a frequency of 5 Hz, which obtained current response correlate inversely with the binding affinity of MIP to Aβ42.

The surface morphology of the electrode modification with graphene, NCD-G, MIP/NCD-G and NIP/NCD-G was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) as shown in Fig. 1 A–D, respectively. Graphene sheet showed wrinkles and smooth surface. In the NCD-G image, small spherical of CD was well distributed on the graphene sheet compared with pure graphene. It confirmed the adsorption of NCD on graphene via π-π interaction force which is usually found on carbon based materials. MIP was electropolymerized by pyrrole as a monomer and Aβ42 as a template, a smooth and uniform polymer film on the NCD-G/SPCE surface structure was observed compared with the NCD-G/SPCE. Fig. 1C and D showed MIP and NIP after the removal of the Aβ42 template. The MIP electrode surface morphology has more surface porous and rougher due to the formation of imprinted cavities, which implies that the template molecules were successfully eluted from the PPY polymer film. Compared with the MIP/NCD-G, the surface of the non-molecular imprinting polymer (NIP), the NIP/NCD-G was much smoother uniform surface covered with polymer without cavities site, indicating the absence of specific imprinted sites. These results suggested that the molecularly imprinted caves were successfully coated on the surface of NCD-G/SPCE. TEM (Transmission electron microscopy) was also used to study the morphology of NCD as shown in Fig. 1E and F. NCD displays a distribution of uniform spherical shape with the average diameters of particle size of about 3.8 to 5.4 nm. This confirms that NCD was successfully prepared.

The UV–vis spectroscopy technique was applied to study the optical properties and the surface passivation of NCD as shown in Fig. 2 A. Two strong absorption peaks were observed. The spectrum at 230 nm was related to the π–π* transition of CC bonds of the aromatic sp2-hybridized carbon core. In the energy region at 352 nm and the weak absorption peak at 426 nm were attributed to the n–π* transition of oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups (CO bonds and C–N bonds) on the NCD surface. This is a non-conjugated electron orbital that originated from the surface and is similar to the previous studies [32–34]. The result confirms the presence of nitrogen atoms functionalized in the carbon-dot structure.

XPS has been employed to study the elemental composition of NCD as shown in Fig. 3 . Three characteristic peaks of the XPS spectra binding energies C 1s, N 1s and O 1s were located at 285 eV, 400 eV, and 533 eV, indicating carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements in the NCD structure. The C1s spectrum reveals three main peaks at 284.83 eV, 285.63 eV and 286.55 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbon of C–C/CC, C–O/C–N and CO, respectively [35,36]. The high-resolution spectra of O1s presented two peaks located at 531.96 and 533.6 eV that corresponded to C–O/N–O and CO/NO bonding, respectively [36]. N1s exhibits two peaks at 399.12 eV and 400.3 eV, indicating that the nitrogen elements on the surface are attributed to C–N and N–H groups, due to the forms of pyrrolic N and graphitic N (N–H). Nitrogen doping can lead to the incorporation of N into the carbon dots in the form of pyrrolic N and graphitic N which can contribute to the π electron system. These XPS experiment results have been similar to the previously reported [37]. This confirms that the doping of nitrogen on CD functionalized with hydroxyl (–OH), amino (–NH2), carboxyl/carbonyl moieties (–COOH) on the surface.

The electrochemical behavior of different modified electrodes was investigated by cyclic volytammetry (CV) in the range from −0.6 V to + 0.9 V at a scan rate of 100 mV/s in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.0 containing 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as the redox probe. In Fig. 2B, the CV curves at the bare SPCE (a), G/SPCE (b), NCD/SPCE (c) and NCD-G/SPCE (d) were presented as a pair of well-defined redox characteristic peaks that corresponded to the electrochemical oxidation and reduction of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Among other redox peaks, the bare SPCE had the lowest peak value while a redox peak current of NCD-G/SPCE was remarkably increased by about two times compared to bare (curve a) and G/SPCE (curve b), indicating the NCD-G has an excellent electrocatalytic property, which accelerates electron transfer at the electrode. The observation is that graphene not only improved the conductive properties but also played an important role in providing the bridges for the electron transfer of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− to the electrode surface. NCD provides a higher active surface area-to-volume ratio. The compared current response before and after rebinding of Aβ42 at both MIP and non-imprinted (NIP) film modified electrodes as MIP/NCD-G/SPCE and NIP/NCD-G/SPCE were investigated by SWV and shown in Fig. 4 A and B. Subsequently, after removing the Aβ42 template from MIP/NCD-G/SPCE, the peak current increased greatly than after polymerization. This indicated that the Aβ42 template was broken off from the imprinted site and provided cavities to produce highly selective recognition sites and made a hole for electron transfer suggesting the successful formation of MIP film. The current response was decreased after rebinding of Aβ42, due to Aβ42 being non-conductive and acting as an insulator, which blocked the electron between the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution and the electrode surface. Conversely, a very small current response at NIP/NCD-G/SPCE before and after binding of Aβ42 was observed. It is clearly seen that the current of the MIP was significantly greater than that of the NIP modified electrode due to no imprint cavities at the NIP. This phenomenon implied the imprinted cavities were produced on the electrode surface not only possesses excellent molecular reorganization property but also shows high imprinted efficiency specific to Aβ42.

The parameter factors of MIP preparation influence the current response of the prepared electrode toward Aβ42 molecules. In order to achieve the maximum sensitivity of the sensor, some factors including the NCD-G concentrations, the polymerization cycles, the ratio of monomer to template molecule, elution time and incubation time were evaluated and optimized by SWV technique at MIP/NCD-G/SPCE to 100 pg/mL of Aβ42 in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.0 containing 2.5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−.

The volume of the NCD-G solution dropped onto the SPCE surface is one significant parameter because it directly affects the nanohybrid thickness. Four different volumes of 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 mg/mL were tested. From the SWV results in Fig. 5 A and B, the obtained peak current at a concentration of NCD-G 0.1 mg/mL had the highest value. Therefore, 0.1 mg/mL was chosen as the optimum amount in further experiments. An excessive or insufficient amount of NCD-G at 0.3 and 0.5 mg/mL leads to the formation of a blocking layer at the electrode surface thus the electron transfer cannot occur properly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the molar ratio of template to monomer
ce:section.ce:para: The monomer to template ratio is effect by the amount of imprinted sites in the polymer matrix that recognizes the capabilities of the MIP sensor. Fig. 5C displays the effect of different ratios of pyrrole monomer and Aβ42 template concentration on the current response of MIP/NCD-G/SPCE to Aβ42 at 4:5, 4:10, 4:15, 4:20 and 4:25. The polypyrrole film was formed on NCD-G/SPCE by CV between +0.0 and +1.4 V. The peak current response of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− to Aβ42 was increased with increasing the monomer to template ratio continuously from 4:5 to 4:15 then declined at 4:20 to 4:25. The excess of template molecules might lead to an aggregate of proteins, resulting in improper and reduced formation of recognition sites or binding cavities in the MIP film. As a result, the ratio of the monomer to the template was selected as 4:15.

The number of electropolymerization cycles of the polymer on the NCD-G/SPCE surface can indicate the thickness of the PPY film and its effect on the sensor sensitivity. The cycle numbers of 2, 3, 5 and 7 cycles were optimized by SWV. The plot of ΔI versus the number of cycles showed in Fig. 5D that ΔI was reached to the maximum at 3 cycles after increasing the number of cycles from 3 to 7. The cycle numbers of more than 3 cycles could produce too thick PPY layers which would make it difficult to completely remove the template molecule and become an insulator at the electrode surface, consequent to a reduction of ΔI. Thus, 3 cycles were given the maximum peak current response for polymerizing the MIP films, indicating the formation of optimal imprinting sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of elution time and rebinding time
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 5E and F, the influence of elution time and rebinding time on template molecules to the current response was investigated by SWV technique. The elution time was increased from 10, 15, 20 and 25 min. The current response increased continuously, reaching its highest at 20 min afterwards, the current decreased, suggesting that the Aβ42 template molecules were removed by disconnecting the hydrogen bond and removing Aβ42. However, extending time may destroy the surface of the polymer film. Therefore, 10 min was chosen as the optimal elution time.

The rebinding time of the sensor was investigated by incubating the Aβ42 solution onto the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE in different periods of time at 1, 5, 10, and 15 min. The current response had continuously increased to the maximum point at 10 min, then slightly decreased, which can implied that the adsorption of Aβ42 was completely binding in MIP cavities and reached saturation at 15 min due to the electron transfer of the redox probe at the electrode surface having been blocked when the cavity site was empty. Hence, 10 min was chosen as the most appropriate rebinding time for efficient binding.

The reproducibility is a key parameter to evaluate the applicability of electrochemical sensors. MIP/NCD-G/SPCE was evaluated with 15 different electrodes prepared by the same procedure under the same conditions of the individual and applied for Aβ42 determination of 100 pg/mL. In Figs. S1 and 6 A, it can be seen from the SWV curves that the plot of current of each electrode remained almost the same and the current signals showed similar values with no significant changes. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained as 2.08 %, indicating that the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE sensor had excellent reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve for electrochemical determination of Aβ42
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6B and C exhibited the SWV voltammograms and the calibration curve from the difference of the peak current ΔI (µA) of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with various Aβ42 concentrations of 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50, 70, and 100 pg/mL at MIP/NCD-G/SPCE. The current response was proportionally increased with increasing the concentrations of Aβ42 from 5 pg/mL and reached a saturation point at a concentration of 100 pg/mL, which was attributed to the rebinding of Aβ42 to the imprinting cavity sites. The linear range of 5 to 70 pg/mL was obtained with the linear regression equations of y = 0.0684x + 1.1686 (the squared correlation coefficient of 0.996). The limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) was calculated to be 1 pg/mL based on three times the blank standard deviation divided by the calibration curve slope (3Sd/m) for ten replicates of blank measurements and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 3.10 pg/mL. The previously reported MIP based on electrochemical Aβ42 sensor performance was summarized in Table S1 in Supplementary information. The MIP sensor in this research work showed a good linear range with a low detection limit. The good sensitivity of this sensor can be attributed to two factors: Firstly, the excellent electrical conductivity of graphene could enhance the electron transfer with NCD assistance. NCD prevented aggregation of graphene sheets, facilitated electron transfer, increased the effective surface area and provided abundant active sites. The specific recognition sites of the MIP matrix exhibit highly affinity for the Aβ42, which could enhance the sensitivity and selectivity.

The selectivity of the proposed MIP/NCD-G/SPCE to Aβ42 was evaluated, and the current responses of 10-fold concentration in the presence of possible proteins that have similar structures to Aβ42, including brain natriuretic peptide (BNP), immunoglobulin G (IgG) and human serum albumin (HSA) were measured and then compared with Aβ42. The signal responses of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− on the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE after incubation of each interference are shown in Fig. 6D. The ΔI value of the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE toward Aβ42 was 5.7 μA, which was 10 times higher than those interferences. The results indicate that the proposed MIP/NCD-G/SPCE has a good selectivity toward Aβ42 due to the recognition sites of the MIP matrix which match the Aβ42 structure in space size and chemical function but do not match these interferences. Therefore, Aβ42 binds more effectively to the imprinted cavity than the above interferences, facilitating accurate determination of Aβ42. However, the MIP cavity designed for Aβ42 may also exhibit some interaction with peptides of similar sequences, such as Aβ40, due to the similarity of the MIP’s recognition sites. Nonetheless, the MIP sensor’s ability to distinguish between these two peptides depends on the specific MIP template used which the current response for Aβ40 may not be as pronounced as that observed for Aβ42.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of the MIP/NCD-G/SPCE in artificial human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The components commonly found in serum samples can interfere with the sensor's performance in determining Aβ42, making it difficult to achieve accurate and reliable measurements in the presence of diverse interference. The standard addition method was used to determine the recovery of Aβ42 in artificial human serum using the SWV technique to evaluate the accuracy and practicality of the proposed MIP/NCD-G/SPCE. The currents response of SWV were studied by subsequently varying concentrations of 0, 10, 20, and 50 pg/mL of Aβ42 were introduced into the prepared artificial human serum samples. The observed recovery values ranged from 92.31 to 119.25 %, and the RSD was less than 5.44 % as the results were shown in Table 1 . These results indicate that the MIP sensor was accurate for detection of Aβ42 in real samples.

This study successfully demonstrated the fabrication of a MIP sensor through the synthesis of an NCD-G nanohybrid-modified SPCE and the electro-polymerization of PPY to form the MIP layer in the presence of an Aβ42 template. The resulting MIP sensor exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and reproducibility, along with a broad linear range and a low limit of detection. Graphene sheets enhance the sensitivity of the sensor by increasing both the conductivity and the active surface area of the electrode. Additionally, NCD not only improve electrical conductivity but also facilitate electrocatalytic reactions due to the rapid electron transfer capabilities. The multiple uses of MIP/NCD-G/SPCE might lead to decreased sensor performance due to the imprints not being effectively reactivated or cleaned. However, since SPCE is designed for single use, this issue in this work is mitigated. This proposed modified electrode offers disposable, low-cost which has great potential for diagnosing Alzheimer’s disease for clinical applications. While MIP selectivity of Aβ42 might be influenced by the presence of structurally similar compounds, leading to potential cross-reactivity. Consequently, addressing the limitations in selectivity of this MIP sensor will still be challenging for further investigation in future studies.

Saithip Pakapongpan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yingyot Poo-arporn: Formal analysis, Data curation. Sunatcha Ninket: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rungtiva P. Poo-arporn: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has received funding support from the NSRF via the Program Management Unit for Human Resources & Institutional Development, Research and Innovation [grant number B05F640135] and National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) and King Mongkut's University of Technology Thonburi: N42A650316 .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111559.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
